𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twelve — Part One

The Book of Shemu'el Aleph

SHEMU'EL ALEPH

The First Book of Samuel

Chapters 1 through 10

Khannah — Eli — Shemu'el — The Ark — The Monarchy Established — Sha'ul Anointed

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QSama (4Q51) — Cave 4 — Primary DSS witness — older text tradition than Masoretic

4QSamb (4Q52) — Cave 4 — Supporting DSS fragments

4QSamc (4Q53) — Cave 4 — Supporting DSS fragments

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — Shemu'el preserved in full

YHWH and The Creator only — no substitute titles

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 10 — KHANNAH THROUGH SHA'UL ANOINTED

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 1 Khannah's Prayer — YHWH Remembered Her — She Called His Name Shemu'el

1 There was a certain man of Ramatayim-Tzofim of the hill country of Efrayim whose name was Elkanah the son of Yerokham, son of Elihu, son of Tokhu, son of Tzuf, an Efrathite.

2 He had two wives. The name of the one was Khannah, and the name of the other Peninah. And Peninah had children, but Khannah had no children.

3 Now this man used to go up year by year from his city to worship and to sacrifice to YHWH of hosts at Shiloh, where the two sons of Eli, Khofni and Pinkhas, were priests of YHWH.

4 On the day when Elkanah sacrificed, he would give portions to Peninah his wife and to all her sons and daughters.

5 But to Khannah he gave a double portion, because he loved her, though YHWH had closed her womb.

6 And her rival used to provoke her grievously to irritate her, because YHWH had closed her womb.

7 So it went on year by year. As often as she went up to the house of YHWH, she used to provoke her. Therefore Khannah wept and would not eat.

8 And Elkanah, her husband, said to her, Khannah, why do you weep? And why do you not eat? And why is your heart sad? Am I not more to you than ten sons?

9 After they had eaten and drunk in Shiloh, Khannah rose. Now Eli the priest was sitting on the seat beside the doorpost of the temple of YHWH.

10 She was deeply distressed and prayed to YHWH and wept bitterly.

11 And she vowed a vow and said, O YHWH of hosts, if you will indeed look on the affliction of your servant and remember me and not forget your servant, but will give to your servant a son, then I will give him to YHWH all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head.

12 As she continued praying before YHWH, Eli observed her mouth.

13 Khannah was speaking in her heart — only her lips moved, and her voice was not heard. Therefore Eli took her to be a drunken woman.

14 And Eli said to her, How long will you go on being drunk? Put your wine away from you.

15 But Khannah answered, No, my lord, I am a woman troubled in spirit. I have drunk neither wine nor strong drink, but I have been pouring out my soul before YHWH.

16 Do not regard your servant as a worthless woman, for all along I have been speaking out of my great anxiety and vexation.

17 Then Eli answered, Go in peace, and the Creator of Yisra'EL grant your petition that you have made to him.

18 And she said, Let your servant find favor in your eyes. Then the woman went her way and ate, and her face was no longer sad.

19 They rose early in the morning and worshiped before YHWH. Then they went back to their house at Ramah. And Elkanah knew Khannah his wife, and YHWH remembered her.

20 And in due time Khannah conceived and bore a son, and she called his name Shemu'el, for she said, I have asked for him from YHWH.

21 The man Elkanah and all his house went up to offer to YHWH the yearly sacrifice and to pay his vow.

22 But Khannah did not go up, for she said to her husband, As soon as the child is weaned, I will bring him, so that he may appear in the presence of YHWH and dwell there forever.

23 Elkanah her husband said to her, Do what seems best to you — wait until you have weaned him. Only may YHWH establish his word. So the woman remained and nursed her son until she weaned him.

24 And when she had weaned him, she took him up with her, along with a three-year-old bull, an ephah of flour, and a skin of wine, and she brought him to the house of YHWH at Shiloh. And the child was young.

25 Then they slaughtered the bull, and they brought the child to Eli.

26 And she said, Oh, my lord. As you live, my lord, I am the woman who was standing here in your presence, praying to YHWH.

27 For this child I prayed, and YHWH has granted me my petition that I made to him.

28 Therefore I have lent him to YHWH. As long as he lives, he is lent to YHWH. And he worshiped YHWH there.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51), 4QSamb (4Q52), and 4QSamc (4Q53) from Cave 4 are the primary DSS witnesses for Shemu'el Aleph and Bet. 4QSama is one of the most significant DSS biblical manuscripts — it preserves an older Hebrew text tradition than the Masoretic text and is frequently closer to the Septuagint. Chapter 1 establishes the covenant pattern of the barren woman whose son becomes YHWH's instrument: Sarah, Rivkah, Rakhel, Manoakh's wife, and now Khannah.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Shemu'el Aleph in full. Khannah's vow in verse 11 — if you will give your servant a son, I will give him to YHWH all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head — is read as the covenant of complete consecration: the most desired gift immediately returned to the Giver.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The DSS 4QSama preserves a text of Shemu'el that frequently differs from the Masoretic and often aligns with the Septuagint. Chapter 1 is well preserved in the DSS tradition. No significant variants affecting the substance of Chapter 1.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — if you will give your servant a son, I will give him to YHWH all the days of his life. Khannah's prayer is the covenant transaction at the opening of Shemu'el: she asks for what she most desires and immediately consecrates it. The Nazirite dedication — no razor shall touch his head — echoes Shimshon's birth announcement in Shofetim 13:5. But where Shimshon's parents received the covenant instruction passively, Khannah initiates it. Verse 20 — YHWH remembered her. The Hebrew zakhar — the covenant remembering that recurs throughout the Hebrew canon from Noakh to Avraham to Yisra'EL in Egypt. YHWH's remembering is always active intervention.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 2 The Song of Khannah — The Sons of Eli — YHWH's Warning Against the House of Eli

1 And Khannah prayed and said, My heart exults in YHWH — my horn is exalted in YHWH. My mouth derides my enemies, because I rejoice in your salvation.

2 There is none holy like YHWH — for there is none besides you — there is no rock like our Creator.

3 Talk no more so very proudly — let not arrogance come from your mouth, for YHWH is a Creator of knowledge, and by him actions are weighed.

4 The bows of the mighty are broken, but the feeble bind on strength.

5 Those who were full have hired themselves out for bread, but those who were hungry have ceased to hunger. The barren has borne seven, but she who has many children is forlorn.

6 YHWH kills and brings to life — he brings down to Sheol and raises up.

7 YHWH makes poor and makes rich — he brings low and he exalts.

8 He raises up the poor from the dust — he lifts the needy from the ash heap to make them sit with princes and inherit a seat of honor. For the pillars of the earth are YHWH's, and on them he has set the world.

9 He will guard the feet of his faithful ones, but the wicked shall be cut off in darkness, for not by might shall a man prevail.

10 The adversaries of YHWH shall be broken to pieces — against them he will thunder in heaven. YHWH will judge the ends of the earth — he will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed.

11 Then Elkanah went home to Ramah. And the boy was ministering to YHWH in the presence of Eli the priest.

12 Now the sons of Eli were worthless men. They did not know YHWH.

13 The custom of the priests with the people was that when any man offered sacrifice, the priest's servant would come, while the meat was boiling, with a three-pronged fork in his hand,

14 And he would thrust it into the pan or kettle or cauldron or pot. All that the fork brought up the priest would take for himself. This is what they did at Shiloh to all the Yisra'ELites who came there.

15 Moreover, before the fat was burned, the priest's servant would come and say to the man who was sacrificing, Give meat for the priest to roast, for he will not accept boiled meat from you but only raw.

16 And if the man said to him, Let them burn the fat first, and then take as much as you wish, he would say, No, you must give it now, and if not, I will take it by force.

17 Thus the sin of the young men was very great in the sight of YHWH, for the men treated the offering of YHWH with contempt.

18 Shemu'el was ministering before YHWH, a boy clothed with a linen ephod.

19 And his mother used to make for him a little robe and take it to him each year when she went up with her husband to offer the yearly sacrifice.

20 Then Eli would bless Elkanah and his wife, and say, May YHWH give you children by this woman for the petition she asked of YHWH. So then they would return to their home.

21 Indeed YHWH visited Khannah, and she conceived and bore three sons and two daughters. And the boy Shemu'el grew in the presence of YHWH.

22 Now Eli was very old, and he kept hearing all that his sons were doing to all Yisra'EL, and how they lay with the women who were serving at the entrance to the tent of meeting.

23 And he said to them, Why do you do such things? For I hear of your evil dealings from all the people.

24 No, my sons — it is no good report that I hear the people of YHWH spreading.

25 If someone sins against a man, Creator will mediate for him, but if someone sins against YHWH, who can intercede for him? But they would not listen to the voice of their father, for it was the will of YHWH to put them to death.

26 Now the boy Shemu'el continued to grow both in stature and in favor with YHWH and also with man.

27 And there came a man of Creator to Eli and said to him, Thus says YHWH, Did I indeed reveal myself to the house of your father when they were in Egypt subject to the house of Pharaoh?

28 Did I choose him out of all the tribes of Yisra'EL to be my priest, to go up to my altar, to burn incense, to wear an ephod before me? I gave to the house of your father all my offerings by fire from the people of Yisra'EL.

29 Why then do you scorn my sacrifices and my offerings that I commanded for my dwelling, and honor your sons above me by fattening yourselves on the choicest parts of every offering of my people Yisra'EL?

30 Therefore YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, declares: I promised that your house and the house of your father should go in and out before me forever, but now YHWH declares: Far be it from me, for those who honor me I will honor, and those who despise me shall be lightly esteemed.

31 Behold, the days are coming when I will cut off your strength and the strength of your father's house, so that there will not be an old man in your house.

32 Then in distress you will look with envious eye on all the prosperity that shall be bestowed on Yisra'EL, and there shall not be an old man in your house forever.

33 The only one of you whom I shall not cut off from my altar shall be spared to weep his eyes out to grieve his heart, and all the descendants of your house shall die by the sword of men.

34 And this that shall come upon your two sons, Khofni and Pinkhas, shall be the sign to you: both of them shall die on the same day.

35 And I will raise up for myself a faithful priest, who shall do according to what is in my heart and in my mind. And I will build him a sure house, and he shall go in and out before my anointed forever.

36 And everyone who is left in your house shall come to implore him for a piece of silver or a loaf of bread and shall say, Please put me in one of the priests' places, that I may eat a morsel of bread.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 2. The Song of Khannah — verses 1-10 — is one of the oldest covenant hymns in the Nevi'im and the literary model for the Magnificat tradition. Verse 8 — he raises up the poor from the dust, he lifts the needy from the ash heap — is identical in theology to Psalm 113:7-8.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. The covenant reversal theology of Khannah's song — the mighty broken, the feeble strengthened, the full hungry, the hungry satisfied, the barren bearing seven — is read as the foundational statement of YHWH's covenant character: he inverts the human hierarchy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves a text of Shemu'el that often differs from the Masoretic. The DSS text is generally considered the more original reading. Verse 10 — YHWH will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed — is the first explicit mention of the king and the anointed in the Nevi'im narrative, establishing the Davidic theology before Dawid appears.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25 — if someone sins against YHWH, who can intercede for him? But they would not listen to the voice of their father, for it was the will of YHWH to put them to death. The hardening of Eli's sons parallels the hardening of Pharaoh: YHWH uses the stubbornness that already exists to accomplish the covenant judgment already determined. Verse 30 — those who honor me I will honor, and those who despise me shall be lightly esteemed. This is the most concentrated statement of covenant reciprocity in Shemu'el: the treatment of YHWH determines how YHWH treats in return. Eli honored his sons above YHWH — verse 29. YHWH will esteem Eli's house lightly in return.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 3 YHWH Calls Shemu'el — Speak Lord Your Servant Hears — A Prophet Established Over All Yisra'EL

1 Now the boy Shemu'el was ministering to YHWH in the presence of Eli. And the word of YHWH was rare in those days — there was no frequent vision.

2 At that time Eli, whose eyesight had begun to grow dim so that he could not see, was lying down in his own place.

3 The lamp of Creator had not yet gone out, and Shemu'el was lying down in the temple of YHWH, where the ark of Creator was.

4 Then YHWH called Shemu'el, and he said, Here I am.

5 And he ran to Eli and said, Here I am, for you called me. But he said, I did not call — lie down again. So he went and lay down.

6 And YHWH called again, Shemu'el. And Shemu'el arose and went to Eli and said, Here I am, for you called me. But he said, I did not call, my son — lie down again.

7 Now Shemu'el did not yet know YHWH, and the word of YHWH had not yet been revealed to him.

8 And YHWH called Shemu'el again the third time. And he arose and went to Eli and said, Here I am, for you called me. Then Eli perceived that YHWH was calling the boy.

9 Therefore Eli said to Shemu'el, Go, lie down, and if he calls you, you shall say, Speak, YHWH, for your servant hears. So Shemu'el went and lay down in his place.

10 And YHWH came and stood, calling as at other times, Shemu'el, Shemu'el. And Shemu'el said, Speak, for your servant hears.

11 Then YHWH said to Shemu'el, Behold, I am about to do a thing in Yisra'EL at which the two ears of everyone who hears it will tingle.

12 On that day I will fulfill against Eli all that I have spoken concerning his house, from beginning to end.

13 And I declare to him that I am about to punish his house forever, for the iniquity that he knew, because his sons were blaspheming Creator, and he did not restrain them.

14 Therefore I swear to the house of Eli that the iniquity of Eli's house shall not be atoned for by sacrifice or offering forever.

15 Shemu'el lay until morning — then he opened the doors of the house of YHWH. And Shemu'el was afraid to tell the vision to Eli.

16 But Eli called Shemu'el and said, Shemu'el, my son. And he said, Here I am.

17 And Eli said, What was it that he told you? Do not hide it from me. May Creator do so to you and more also if you hide anything from me of all that he told you.

18 So Shemu'el told him everything and hid nothing from him. And he said, It is YHWH. Let him do what seems good to him.

19 And Shemu'el grew, and YHWH was with him and let none of his words fall to the ground.

20 And all Yisra'EL from Dan to Be'er-Sheva knew that Shemu'el was established as a prophet of YHWH.

21 And YHWH appeared again at Shiloh, for YHWH revealed himself to Shemu'el at Shiloh by the word of YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 3. The calling of Shemu'el — three times YHWH calls and three times the boy runs to Eli — is the covenant calling narrative of the Nevi'im. The word of YHWH was rare — verse 1 — establishes the prophetic drought that Shemu'el's calling will end.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Eli's response to the devastating word of judgment — verse 18 — it is YHWH. Let him do what seems good to him — is read as the covenant model of submission to divine judgment: the one who receives the word of condemnation accepts it without defense.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves a version of Chapter 3 consistent with but slightly different from the Masoretic. Verse 1 — the word of YHWH was rare — is one of the most significant statements about prophetic silence in the Nevi'im. YHWH's word is not always present — its absence is itself a covenant statement.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — the word of YHWH was rare in those days — there was no frequent vision. The opening of Shemu'el's prophetic career begins with the diagnosis of prophetic famine. YHWH has been silent. The covenant community has been without the word. Into this silence YHWH speaks — to a child, in the night, mistaken for a human voice. Verse 9 — Speak, YHWH, for your servant hears. The posture Eli teaches Shemu'el is the covenant posture of the prophet: not to speak but to hear. Verse 19 — YHWH let none of his words fall to the ground. The same image as the covenant faithfulness of Yehoshua 21:45 and 23:14 — YHWH's words do not fall wasted. The prophet whose words do not fall to the ground reflects the character of the One who sent him.

THE ARK NARRATIVE — CHAPTERS 4 THROUGH 7

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 4 The Ark Captured — Eli's Sons Die — Eli Falls and Dies — Ikhavod — The Glory Has Departed

1 And the word of Shemu'el came to all Yisra'EL. Now Yisra'EL went out to battle against the Pelishtim. They encamped at Even-ha-Ezer, and the Pelishtim encamped at Afek.

2 The Pelishtim drew up in line against Yisra'EL, and when the battle spread, Yisra'EL was defeated before the Pelishtim, who killed about four thousand men on the field of battle.

3 And when the people came to the camp, the elders of Yisra'EL said, Why has YHWH defeated us today before the Pelishtim? Let us bring the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH here from Shiloh, that it may come among us and save us from the power of our enemies.

4 So the people sent to Shiloh and brought from there the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH of hosts, who is enthroned on the cherubim. And the two sons of Eli, Khofni and Pinkhas, were there with the Ark of the Covenant of Creator.

5 As soon as the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH came into the camp, all Yisra'EL gave a mighty shout, so that the earth resounded.

6 And when the Pelishtim heard the noise of the shouting, they said, What does this great shouting in the camp of the Hebrews mean? And when they learned that the Ark of YHWH had come to the camp,

7 The Pelishtim were afraid, for they said, A god has come into the camp. And they said, Woe to us. For nothing like this has happened before.

8 Woe to us. Who can deliver us from the power of these mighty gods? These are the gods who struck the Egyptians with every sort of plague in the wilderness.

9 Take courage, and be men, O Pelishtim, lest you become slaves to the Hebrews as they have been to you. Be men and fight.

10 So the Pelishtim fought, and Yisra'EL was defeated, and they fled, every man to his home. And there was a very great slaughter, for thirty thousand foot soldiers of Yisra'EL fell.

11 And the Ark of Creator was captured, and the two sons of Eli, Khofni and Pinkhas, died.

12 A man of Binyamin ran from the battle line and came to Shiloh the same day, with his clothes torn and with dirt on his head.

13 When he arrived, Eli was sitting on his seat by the road watching, for his heart trembled for the Ark of Creator. And when the man came into the city and told the news, all the city cried out.

14 When Eli heard the sound of the outcry, he said, What is this uproar? Then the man hurried and came and told Eli.

15 Now Eli was ninety-eight years old and his eyes were set so that he could not see.

16 And the man said to Eli, I am he who has come from the battle — I fled from the battle today. And he said, How did it go, my son?

17 He who brought the news answered and said, Yisra'EL has fled before the Pelishtim, and there has also been a great defeat among the people. Your two sons also, Khofni and Pinkhas, are dead, and the Ark of Creator has been captured.

18 As soon as he mentioned the Ark of Creator, Eli fell over backward from his seat by the side of the gate, and his neck was broken and he died, for the man was old and heavy. He had judged Yisra'EL forty years.

19 Now his daughter-in-law, the wife of Pinkhas, was pregnant, about to give birth. And when she heard the news that the Ark of Creator had been captured, and that her father-in-law and her husband were dead, she bowed and gave birth, for her pains came upon her.

20 And about the time of her death the women attending her said to her, Do not be afraid, for you have borne a son. But she did not answer or pay attention.

21 And she named the child Ikhavod, saying, The glory has departed from Yisra'EL, because the Ark of Creator had been captured and because of her father-in-law and her husband.

22 And she said, The glory has departed from Yisra'EL, for the Ark of Creator has been captured.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 4. The capture of the Ark is the covenant catastrophe of the Shiloh period. The Ark — YHWH's covenant throne between the cherubim — is taken by the Pelishtim. The covenant community had treated it as a magical talisman rather than the covenant presence of YHWH.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 21-22 — Ikhavod — the glory has departed from Yisra'EL — is read as the covenant declaration that YHWH's presence cannot be compelled or possessed. When the covenant community treats the Ark as an object to be deployed rather than the presence of YHWH to be honored, the Ark leaves.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves a text of Chapter 4 consistent with the Masoretic tradition. The death of Eli — verse 18 — falling backward from his seat when he hears the Ark is taken — is the fulfillment of the covenant word delivered in Chapter 2.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — let us bring the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH here that it may come among us and save us. The elders of Yisra'EL treat the Ark as a covenant mechanism that guarantees victory if present. This is the error Shofetim documented: the covenant people replacing covenant faithfulness with covenant objects. The Ark is not a weapon — it is the throne of YHWH. His presence goes with it only when the covenant is honored. Verse 21 — Ikhavod — the glory has departed. The dying woman names the theological reality that the military defeat only expressed. The glory — kavod — YHWH's covenant presence — departed when the covenant was dishonored. The Ark's capture is the physical sign of a spiritual reality that preceded it.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 5 The Ark Among the Pelishtim — Dagon Falls Before It — Tumors Upon the People

1 When the Pelishtim captured the Ark of Creator, they brought it from Even-ha-Ezer to Ashdod.

2 Then the Pelishtim took the Ark of Creator and brought it into the house of Dagon and set it up beside Dagon.

3 And when the people of Ashdod rose early the next day, behold, Dagon had fallen face downward on the ground before the Ark of YHWH. So they took Dagon and put him back in his place.

4 But when they rose early on the next morning, behold, Dagon had fallen face downward on the ground before the Ark of YHWH, and the head of Dagon and both his hands were lying cut off on the threshold. Only the trunk of Dagon was left to him.

5 This is why the priests of Dagon and all who enter the house of Dagon do not tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod to this day.

6 The hand of YHWH was heavy against the people of Ashdod, and he terrified and afflicted them with tumors, both Ashdod and its territory.

7 And when the men of Ashdod saw how things were, they said, The Ark of the Creator of Yisra'EL must not remain with us, for his hand is hard against us and against Dagon our god.

8 So they sent and gathered together all the lords of the Pelishtim and said, What shall we do with the Ark of the Creator of Yisra'EL? They answered, Let the Ark of the Creator of Yisra'EL be brought around to Gat. So they brought the Ark of the Creator of Yisra'EL there.

9 But after they had brought it around, the hand of YHWH was against the city, causing a very great panic, and he afflicted the men of the city, both young and old, so that tumors broke out on them.

10 So they sent the Ark of Creator to Ekron. But as soon as the Ark of Creator came to Ekron, the people of Ekron cried out, They have brought around to us the Ark of the Creator of Yisra'EL to kill us and our people.

11 They sent therefore and gathered together all the lords of the Pelishtim and said, Send away the Ark of the Creator of Yisra'EL, and let it return to its own place, that it may not kill us and our people. For there was a deathly panic throughout the whole city. The hand of Creator was very heavy there.

12 The men who did not die were struck with tumors, and the cry of the city went up to heaven.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 5. The Ark's journey through the five Pelishti cities is the covenant demonstration that YHWH is not a tribal deity whose power is confined to Yisra'EL's territory. In the heart of enemy territory, YHWH overthrows Dagon's idol and afflicts those who took the Ark.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. The falling of Dagon — first face down, then with head and hands cut off — is read as the covenant declaration that YHWH cannot be placed alongside other gods. The Ark does not coexist with Dagon. It destroys him.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves a version of Chapter 5 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The tumors afflicting the Pelishtim are identified in later manuscript traditions as hemorrhoids — the nature of the plague connects to the covenant curse of Devarim 28:27.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3-4 — Dagon had fallen face downward before the Ark of YHWH — they put him back — the next morning Dagon had fallen again and his head and hands were cut off on the threshold. YHWH does not need Yisra'EL's army to demonstrate his sovereignty. In the Pelishti temple, without any covenant community present, YHWH overthrows the idol twice. The second fall is more complete: the head — the seat of intelligence and authority — and the hands — the instruments of power and action — are severed. The trunk — the body without mind or capability — is all that remains of Dagon. This is the covenant theology of the Ark narrative: YHWH's sovereign presence cannot be captured or contained.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 6 The Ark Returns to Yisra'EL — The Men of Beit-Shemesh Struck — Great Is This Holy Creator

1 The Ark of YHWH was in the country of the Pelishtim seven months.

2 And the Pelishtim called for the priests and the diviners and said, What shall we do with the Ark of YHWH? Tell us with what we shall send it to its place.

3 They said, If you send away the Ark of the Creator of Yisra'EL, do not send it empty, but by all means return him a guilt offering. Then you will be healed, and it will be known to you why his hand does not turn away from you.

4 And they said, What is the guilt offering that we shall return to him? They answered, Five golden tumors and five golden mice, according to the number of the lords of the Pelishtim, for the same plague was on all of you and on your lords.

5 So you must make images of your tumors and images of your mice that ravage the land, and give glory to the Creator of Yisra'EL. Perhaps he will lighten his hand from off you and your gods and your land.

6 Why should you harden your hearts as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts? After he had dealt severely with them, did they not send the people away, and they departed?

7 Now then, take and prepare a new cart and two milk cows on which there has never come a yoke, and yoke the cows to the cart, but take their calves home, away from them.

8 And take the Ark of YHWH and place it on the cart and put in a box at its side the figures of gold, which you are returning to him as a guilt offering. Then send it off and let it go its way.

9 And watch. If it goes up on the way to its own land, to Beit-Shemesh, then it is he who has done us this great harm, but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that struck us — it happened to us by coincidence.

10 The men did so, and took two milk cows and yoked them to the cart and shut up their calves at home.

11 And they put the Ark of YHWH on the cart and the box with the golden mice and the images of their tumors.

12 And the cows went straight in the direction of Beit-Shemesh along one highway, lowing as they went. They turned neither to the right nor to the left, and the lords of the Pelishtim went after them as far as the border of Beit-Shemesh.

13 Now the people of Beit-Shemesh were reaping their wheat harvest in the valley. And when they lifted up their eyes and saw the Ark, they rejoiced to see it.

14 The cart came into the field of Yehoshua of Beit-Shemesh and stopped there. A great stone was there. And they split up the wood of the cart and offered the cows as a burnt offering to YHWH.

15 And the Levites took down the Ark of YHWH and the box that was beside it, in which were the golden figures, and set them upon the great stone. And the men of Beit-Shemesh offered burnt offerings and sacrificed sacrifices on that day to YHWH.

16 And when the five lords of the Pelishtim saw it, they returned that day to Ekron.

17 These are the golden tumors that the Pelishtim returned as a guilt offering to YHWH: one for Ashdod, one for Azzah, one for Ashkelon, one for Gat, one for Ekron,

18 And the golden mice, according to the number of all the cities of the Pelishtim belonging to the five lords, both fortified cities and unwalled villages. The great stone beside which they set down the Ark of YHWH is a witness to this day in the field of Yehoshua of Beit-Shemesh.

19 And he struck some of the men of Beit-Shemesh, because they looked upon the Ark of YHWH. He struck seventy men of them, and the people mourned because YHWH had struck the people with a great blow.

20 Then the men of Beit-Shemesh said, Who is able to stand before YHWH, this holy Creator? And to whom shall he go up away from us?

21 So they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kiryat-Ye'arim, saying, The Pelishtim have returned the Ark of YHWH. Come down and take it up to you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 6. The Pelishtim's use of a divination test — untrained cows yoked to a cart, calves removed — is the most theologically ironic episode in the Ark narrative: the enemies of the covenant community design a test that YHWH passes definitively. The cows go straight to Beit-Shemesh without deviation.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. The Pelishti priests' reference to Egypt in verse 6 — why should you harden your hearts as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts? — is read as the covenant awareness that even the nations have learned the lesson of the Exodus: resistance to YHWH's will produces catastrophe.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 6 with some variants from the Masoretic. The number of men struck at Beit-Shemesh — verse 19 — varies between manuscripts. The Masoretic reads fifty thousand seventy. The DSS and other ancient witnesses support seventy as the original reading. This compilation follows the DSS reading of seventy.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — why should you harden your hearts as the Egyptians? The Pelishti priests know the Exodus tradition. The nations have heard and are aware of YHWH's acts — this is consistent with Rakhav's declaration in Yehoshua 2:10-11 and the covenant theology of Psalm 98:2. The cows' straight path — verse 12 — is the covenant sign: YHWH guides untrained animals directly home while overcoming every natural instinct toward their calves. Verse 20 — who is able to stand before YHWH, this holy Creator? The men of Beit-Shemesh ask the right question. YHWH's holiness is not merely a religious attribute — it is the overwhelming reality that judges every approach to him.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 7 Shemu'el Judges Yisra'EL — The Pelishtim Defeated at Mitzpah — Ebenezar — Thus Far YHWH Has Helped Us

1 And the men of Kiryat-Ye'arim came and took up the Ark of YHWH and brought it to the house of Avina'dav on the hill. And they consecrated his son Ele'azar to have charge of the Ark of YHWH.

2 From the day that the ark was lodged at Kiryat-Ye'arim, a long time passed, some twenty years, and all the house of Yisra'EL lamented after YHWH.

3 And Shemu'el said to all the house of Yisra'EL, If you are returning to YHWH with all your heart, then put away the foreign gods and the Ashtarots from among you and direct your heart to YHWH and serve him only, and he will deliver you out of the hand of the Pelishtim.

4 So the people of Yisra'EL put away the Ba'als and the Ashtarots, and they served YHWH only.

5 Then Shemu'el said, Gather all Yisra'EL at Mitzpah, and I will pray to YHWH for you.

6 So they gathered at Mitzpah and drew water and poured it out before YHWH and fasted on that day and said there, We have sinned against YHWH. And Shemu'el judged the people of Yisra'EL at Mitzpah.

7 Now when the Pelishtim heard that the people of Yisra'EL had gathered at Mitzpah, the lords of the Pelishtim went up against Yisra'EL. And when the people of Yisra'EL heard of it, they were afraid of the Pelishtim.

8 And the people of Yisra'EL said to Shemu'el, Do not cease to cry out to YHWH our Creator for us, that he may save us from the hand of the Pelishtim.

9 So Shemu'el took a nursing lamb and offered it as a whole burnt offering to YHWH. And Shemu'el cried out to YHWH for Yisra'EL, and YHWH answered him.

10 As Shemu'el was offering up the burnt offering, the Pelishtim drew near to attack Yisra'EL. But YHWH thundered with a mighty sound that day against the Pelishtim and threw them into confusion, and they were defeated before Yisra'EL.

11 And the men of Yisra'EL went out from Mitzpah and pursued the Pelishtim and struck them, as far as below Beit-Kar.

12 Then Shemu'el took a stone and set it up between Mitzpah and Shen and called its name Even-ha-Ezer — for he said, Thus far YHWH has helped us.

13 So the Pelishtim were subdued and did not again enter the territory of Yisra'EL. And the hand of YHWH was against the Pelishtim all the days of Shemu'el.

14 The cities that the Pelishtim had taken from Yisra'EL were restored to Yisra'EL, from Ekron to Gat, and Yisra'EL delivered their territory from the hand of the Pelishtim. There was also peace between Yisra'EL and the Amorites.

15 Shemu'el judged Yisra'EL all the days of his life.

16 And he went on a circuit year by year to Beit-El, Gilgal, and Mitzpah. And he judged Yisra'EL in all these places.

17 Then he would return to Ramah, for his home was there, and there also he judged Yisra'EL. And he built there an altar to YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 7. The Mitzpah assembly — verse 6 — is the covenant renewal ceremony of Shemu'el's judgeship: drawing water and pouring it out before YHWH, fasting, and confessing sin. The victory against the Pelishtim at Mitzpah reverses the defeat at Even-ha-Ezer in Chapter 4.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. Verse 12 — Shemu'el took a stone and called its name Even-ha-Ezer — thus far YHWH has helped us — is read as the covenant memorial of YHWH's faithfulness. The stone marks the boundary between what YHWH has already accomplished and what he will yet do.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 7 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The victory at Mitzpah reverses the defeat at Even-ha-Ezer — the same name, the same location, the opposite outcome. The difference is covenant faithfulness: in Chapter 4, Yisra'EL brought the Ark without repentance. In Chapter 7, Shemu'el leads the people in genuine repentance before the battle.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — if you are returning to YHWH with all your heart, put away the foreign gods and serve him only. Shemu'el's covenant condition is the same as every covenant call in the Nevi'im: the totality of covenant allegiance — all your heart — expressed in the practical act of putting away the foreign gods. Verse 10 — YHWH thundered with a mighty sound that day against the Pelishtim. YHWH fights directly — as at the Red Sea, as at the Yarden, as in Gid'on's battle. The covenant pattern is consistent: genuine repentance and covenant alignment precede YHWH's direct intervention. Verse 12 — thus far YHWH has helped us. The stone is a covenant marker: not of triumph but of gratitude at a specific moment. Thus far. The acknowledgment that every step of deliverance has been YHWH's help, not Yisra'EL's strength.

THE REQUEST FOR A KING — CHAPTERS 8 THROUGH 10

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 8 Give Us a King — They Have Rejected Me From Being King Over Them — The Warning of the King

1 When Shemu'el became old, he made his sons judges over Yisra'EL.

2 The name of his firstborn son was Yoel, and the name of his second, Aviyah — they were judges in Be'er-Sheva.

3 Yet his sons did not walk in his ways but turned aside after gain. They took bribes and perverted justice.

4 Then all the elders of Yisra'EL gathered together and came to Shemu'el at Ramah

5 And said to him, Behold, you are old and your sons do not walk in your ways. Now appoint for us a king to judge us like all the nations.

6 But the thing displeased Shemu'el when they said, Give us a king to judge us. And Shemu'el prayed to YHWH.

7 And YHWH said to Shemu'el, Obey the voice of the people in all that they say to you, for they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them.

8 According to all the deeds that they have done, from the day I brought them up out of Egypt even to this day, forsaking me and serving other gods, so they are also doing to you.

9 Now then, obey their voice — only you shall solemnly warn them and show them the ways of the king who shall reign over them.

10 So Shemu'el told all the words of YHWH to the people who were asking for a king from him.

11 He said, These will be the ways of the king who will reign over you: he will take your sons and appoint them to his chariots and to be his horsemen and to run before his chariots.

12 And he will appoint for himself commanders of thousands and commanders of fifties, and some to plow his ground and to reap his harvest, and to make his implements of war and the equipment of his chariots.

13 He will take your daughters to be perfumers and cooks and bakers.

14 He will take the best of your fields and vineyards and olive orchards and give them to his servants.

15 He will take the tenth of your grain and of your vineyards and give it to his officers and to his servants.

16 He will take your male servants and female servants and the best of your young men and your donkeys, and put them to his work.

17 He will take the tenth of your flocks, and you shall be his slaves.

18 And in that day you will cry out because of your king, whom you have chosen for yourselves, but YHWH will not answer you in that day.

19 But the people refused to obey the voice of Shemu'el. And they said, No. But there shall be a king over us,

20 That we also may be like all the nations, and that our king may judge us and go out before us and fight our battles.

21 And when Shemu'el had heard all the words of the people, he repeated them in the ears of YHWH.

22 And YHWH said to Shemu'el, Obey their voice and make them a king. Shemu'el then said to the men of Yisra'EL, Go every man to his city.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 8. The request for a king is one of the most theologically significant moments in the entire Nevi'im. YHWH's declaration — they have not rejected you but they have rejected me from being king over them — makes explicit what Gid'on declared in Shofetim 8:23 and what the entire period of Shofetim demonstrated.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 8 in full. Shemu'el's warning — verses 11-18 — cataloging the exactions of a king is read as the covenant prophetic declaration of covenant consequence: the people choosing human kingship over divine kingship will pay the full cost of that choice.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 8 largely consistent with the Masoretic. YHWH's instruction — obey their voice — twice in verses 7 and 22 — is not covenant approval of the monarchy but covenant accommodation to the people's covenant failure. YHWH works within the chosen structure while declaring its inherent limitations.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from being king over them. YHWH identifies the theological nature of the request with precision: it is not a practical administrative request — it is a theological rejection of YHWH's kingship. The desire to be like all the nations — verse 20 — is the covenant summary of the problem. Yisra'EL was called to be unlike the nations — a kingdom of priests, a holy nation — Shemot 19:6. The request for a king is the request to surrender that calling. Yet YHWH accommodates the request while pronouncing its cost in advance. Verses 11-18 are the covenant warning that the people hear and reject. Verse 18 — you will cry out because of your king, whom you have chosen for yourselves, but YHWH will not answer you. The covenant people are given what they demand and will bear the consequences.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 9 Sha'ul Searching for His Father's Donkeys — The Seer — YHWH Has Chosen Him to Be Prince

1 There was a man of Binyamin whose name was Kish, the son of Aviel, son of Tzeror, son of Bekhorat, son of Afiyakh, a Binyaminite, a man of wealth.

2 And he had a son whose name was Sha'ul, a handsome young man. There was not a man among the people of Yisra'EL more handsome than he. From his shoulders upward he was taller than any of the people.

3 Now the donkeys of Kish, Sha'ul's father, were lost. So Kish said to Sha'ul his son, Take one of the young men with you, and arise, go and look for the donkeys.

4 And he passed through the hill country of Efrayim and passed through the land of Shalishah, but they did not find them. And they passed through the land of Sha'alim, but they were not there. Then he passed through the land of Binyamin, but they did not find them.

5 When they came to the land of Tzuf, Sha'ul said to his servant who was with him, Come, let us go back, lest my father cease to care about the donkeys and become anxious about us.

6 But he said to him, Behold, there is a man of Creator in this city, and he is a man who is held in honor. All that he says comes true. So now let us go there. Perhaps he can tell us the way we should go.

7 Then Sha'ul said to his servant, But if we go, what can we bring the man? For the bread in our sacks is gone, and there is no present to bring to the man of Creator. What do we have?

8 The servant answered Sha'ul again, Here, I have with me a quarter of a shekel of silver, and I will give it to the man of Creator to tell us our way.

9 Formerly in Yisra'EL, when a man went to inquire of Creator, he said, Come, let us go to the seer, for today's prophet was formerly called a seer.

10 And Sha'ul said to his servant, Well said — come, let us go. So they went to the city where the man of Creator was.

11 As they went up the hill to the city, they met young women coming out to draw water and said to them, Is the seer here?

12 They answered, He is — behold, he is just ahead of you. Hurry. He has come just now to the city, because the people have a sacrifice today on the high place.

13 As soon as you enter the city you will find him, before he goes up to the high place to eat. For the people will not eat till he comes, since he must bless the sacrifice. After that those who are invited will eat. Now go up, for you will meet him immediately.

14 So they went up to the city. As they were entering the city, they saw Shemu'el coming out toward them on his way up to the high place.

15 Now the day before Sha'ul came, YHWH had revealed to Shemu'el:

16 Tomorrow about this time I will send to you a man from the land of Binyamin, and you shall anoint him to be prince over my people Yisra'EL. He shall save my people from the hand of the Pelishtim. For I have seen my people, because their cry has come to me.

17 When Shemu'el saw Sha'ul, YHWH told him, Here is the man of whom I spoke to you. He it is who shall restrain my people.

18 Then Sha'ul approached Shemu'el in the gate and said, Tell me where is the house of the seer?

19 Shemu'el answered Sha'ul, I am the seer. Go up before me to the high place, for today you shall eat with me, and in the morning I will let you go and will tell you all that is on your mind.

20 As for your donkeys that were lost three days ago, do not set your mind on them, for they have been found. And for whom is all that is desirable in Yisra'EL? Is it not for you and for all your father's house?

21 Sha'ul answered, Am I not a Binyaminite, from the least of the tribes of Yisra'EL? And is not my clan the humblest of all the clans of the tribe of Binyamin? Why then have you spoken to me in this way?

22 Then Shemu'el took Sha'ul and his young man and brought them into the hall and gave them a place at the head of those who had been invited, who were about thirty persons.

23 And Shemu'el said to the cook, Bring the portion I gave you, of which I said to you, Put it aside.

24 So the cook took up the leg and what was on it and set them before Sha'ul. And Shemu'el said, See, what was kept is set before you. Eat, because it was kept for you until the hour appointed, that you might eat with the guests. So Sha'ul ate with Shemu'el that day.

25 And when they came down from the high place into the city, a bed was spread for Sha'ul on the roof, and he lay down to sleep.

26 Then at the break of dawn Shemu'el called to Sha'ul on the roof, Up, that I may send you on your way. So Sha'ul arose, and both he and Shemu'el went out into the street.

27 As they were going down to the outskirts of the city, Shemu'el said to Sha'ul, Tell the servant to pass on before us, and when he has passed on, stop here yourself for a while, that I may make known to you the word of Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 9. The introduction of Sha'ul — searching for lost donkeys, the most ordinary of tasks — and his encounter with Shemu'el is one of the most carefully structured covenant calling narratives in the Nevi'im. YHWH had prepared the encounter the day before Sha'ul arrived.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 9 in full. Verse 16 — I have seen my people, because their cry has come to me — is read as the covenant repetition of the Exodus formula. YHWH's response to the cry of his people is the structural foundation of every act of covenant deliverance from Shemot 3:7 onward.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 9 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 9 — today's prophet was formerly called a seer — is an editorial note explaining a terminological shift in covenant leadership: the institution of the navi — prophet — is replacing the older institution of the ro'eh — seer.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — from his shoulders upward he was taller than any of the people. Sha'ul's physical description is the covenant irony of the entire Sha'ul narrative. The people wanted a king like the nations — visible, impressive, militarily commanding. YHWH gives them exactly the king they imagined. Tall, handsome, from the smallest tribe — humble by background, imposing by appearance. The covenant problem of Sha'ul will not be his stature but his heart. Verse 21 — am I not from the least of the tribes? Sha'ul's humility at the moment of calling recalls Gid'on in Shofetim 6:15 and Moshe in Shemot 3:11. The covenant pattern of calling selects what appears insufficient and invests it with covenant purpose.

SHEMU'EL ALEPH — CHAPTER 10 Sha'ul Anointed as Prince — The Signs Fulfilled — He Was Turned Into Another Man — Sha'ul Chosen by Lot

1 Then Shemu'el took a flask of oil and poured it on his head and kissed him and said, Has not YHWH anointed you to be prince over his people Yisra'EL? And you shall reign over the people of YHWH and you will save them from the hand of their surrounding enemies. And this shall be the sign to you that YHWH has anointed you to be prince over his heritage.

2 When you depart from me today, you will meet two men by Rachel's tomb in the territory of Binyamin at Tzeltzakh, and they will say to you, The donkeys that you went to seek are found, and now your father has ceased to care about the donkeys and is anxious about you, saying, What shall I do about my son?

3 Then you shall go on from there farther and come to the oak of Tavor. Three men going up to Creator at Beit-El will meet you there, one carrying three young goats, another carrying three loaves of bread, and another carrying a skin of wine.

4 And they will greet you and give you two loaves of bread, which you shall accept from their hand.

5 After that you shall come to Giv'at-ha-Elohim, where there is a garrison of the Pelishtim. And there, as soon as you come to the city, you will meet a group of prophets coming down from the high place with harp, tambourine, flute, and lyre before them, prophesying.

6 Then the Spirit of YHWH will rush upon you, and you will prophesy with them and be turned into another man.

7 Now when these signs meet you, do what your hand finds to do, for Creator is with you.

8 Then go down before me to Gilgal. And behold, I am coming down to you to offer burnt offerings and to sacrifice peace offerings. Seven days you shall wait, until I come to you and show you what you shall do.

9 When he turned his back to leave Shemu'el, Creator gave him another heart. And all these signs came to pass that day.

10 When they came to Giv'ah, behold, a group of prophets met him, and the Spirit of Creator rushed upon him, and he prophesied among them.

11 And when all who knew him previously saw how he prophesied with the prophets, the people said to one another, What has come over the son of Kish? Is Sha'ul also among the prophets?

12 And a man of the place answered, And who is their father? Therefore it became a proverb, Is Sha'ul also among the prophets?

13 When he had finished prophesying, he came to the high place.

14 Sha'ul's uncle said to him and to his servant, Where did you go? And he said, To seek the donkeys. And when we saw they were not to be found, we went to Shemu'el.

15 And Sha'ul's uncle said, Please tell me what Shemu'el said to you.

16 And Sha'ul said to his uncle, He told us plainly that the donkeys had been found. But about the matter of the kingdom, of which Shemu'el had spoken, he did not tell him anything.

17 Now Shemu'el called the people together to YHWH at Mitzpah.

18 And he said to the people of Yisra'EL, Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL: I brought up Yisra'EL out of Egypt, and I delivered you from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of all the kingdoms that were oppressing you.

19 But today you have rejected your Creator, who saves you from all your calamities and your distresses, and you have said to him, No, but set a king over us. Now therefore present yourselves before YHWH by your tribes and by your thousands.

20 Then Shemu'el brought all the tribes of Yisra'EL near, and the tribe of Binyamin was taken by lot.

21 He brought the tribe of Binyamin near by its clans, and the clan of Matri was taken by lot. And Sha'ul the son of Kish was taken by lot. But when they sought him, he could not be found.

22 So they inquired again of YHWH, Is there a man still to come? And YHWH said, Behold, he has hidden himself among the baggage.

23 Then they ran and took him from there. And when he stood among the people, he was taller than any of the people from his shoulders upward.

24 And Shemu'el said to all the people, Do you see him whom YHWH has chosen? There is none like him among all the people. And all the people shouted, Long live the king.

25 Then Shemu'el told the people the rights and duties of the kingship, and he wrote them in a book and laid it up before YHWH. Then Shemu'el sent all the people away, each one to his home.

26 Sha'ul also went to his home at Giv'ah, and with him went men of valor whose hearts Creator had touched.

27 But some worthless fellows said, How can this man save us? And they despised him and brought him no present. But he held his peace.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama (4Q51) preserves portions of Chapter 10. The anointing of Sha'ul as prince — verse 1 — uses the word nagid — prince or designated leader — rather than melekh — king. This covenant distinction is significant: YHWH anoints Sha'ul as his designated servant-leader over the people, not as an autonomous king.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 10 in full. Verse 9 — Creator gave him another heart. The internal transformation that accompanies the external anointing is read as the covenant gift of preparation: YHWH equips what he calls. The same principle as the Spirit clothing Gid'on in Shofetim 6:34.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 10 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The lot-casting sequence — tribe by tribe, then clan, then household, then individual — mirrors the lot-casting process of Yehoshua 7:14 when Akhan was identified. The covenant process of identification by lot places the outcome entirely in YHWH's hands.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 21-22 — Sha'ul was taken by lot. But when they sought him, he could not be found. He had hidden himself among the baggage. The king chosen by YHWH, transformed by the Spirit, given another heart — is found hiding among the baggage when the lot falls on him. Humility? Fear? The text does not explain. But the image is the covenant shadow over the entire Sha'ul narrative: the chosen king who hides from his calling. Verse 25 — Shemu'el wrote the rights and duties of the kingship in a book and laid it up before YHWH. The covenant charter of the monarchy is written and deposited before YHWH. The king is accountable to what is written. This is the covenant principle of Devarim 17:18-20 fulfilled.

END OF PART ONE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 10

Volume Twelve — Shemu'el Aleph — Part One

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

CONTINUES IN PART TWO — CHAPTERS 11 THROUGH 17
